samples.title: Black --check returns 1 when invalid config file is specified
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] Black --check returns 1 when invalid config file is specified **Describe the bug** --check returns 1 when invalid --config is specified, not some higher error code as implied. This can make debugging issues tricky for CI integrations relying on return codes. I wouldn't go so far as to call it a bug, but it was a bit unexpected to me. (It also happens without --check.) **To Reproduce** [CODE] **Expected behavior** --check says: So I would expect this case to return an error code other than 1. **Environment (please complete the following information):** - Version: 19.10b0, master (both tested) - OS and Python version: macOS 10.15.3 with Python 3.7.7; and Ubuntu 18.04.4 with Python 3.6.9 **Does this bug also happen on master?** Yes (tested via cloning and pipenv shell) **Additional context** This can be very awkward to debug in a CI environment if you rely on return codes only. [SEP] [COMMENTER] I think this could be quite an easy fix. I'm not sure why, but --config doesn't require the file to exist: [URL] src does: [URL] If exists=True, then this becomes a [BadParameter]( [URL] click exceptions, which inherits from [UsageError]( [URL] which has [exit_code = 2]( [URL] as does any other invalid parameter. Instead, it progresses until it tries to read the config file: [URL] This raises a [FileError]( [URL] which inherits from [ClickException]( [URL] which has [exit_code = 1]( [URL] With exists=True, this happens instead: [CODE] The case where no file is specified still works, too. Happy to create a pull request if this sounds like a good solution to you? [SEP] [COMMENTER] Switching to exists=True sounds like the right call. Please send a PR!

samples.title: HdfsTarget commands fail when many targets are instantiated
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] HdfsTarget commands fail when many targets are instantiated I've recently added an existence check to a large mapreduce task for which some input files may be missing. With a large enough set of inputs, it will fail every time. I've simplified it to the following code: [CODE] This will break if I use any past 1000 or so. Here the client uses snakebite. For a more direct triggering, we can also do [CODE] In either case, the bug goes away if I use a generator expression rather than a list comprehension. The problem is that when I'm dealing with objects coming out of luigi calls like input_hadoop, it's too late for me to decide between lists and iterators. I can code around this by instantiating all of my HdfsTargets with the same client, but I'm not sure this is safe. It could also be fixed in luigi if we had get_autoconfig_client return the same object each time. Is there any reason this wouldn't work? [SEP] [COMMENTER] Thanks for the report. @daveFNbuck, do you understand what causes this? I don't understand how you got to the conclusion that making get_autoconfig_client returning the same objects resolves the issue. Anyhow I'm pretty sure get_autoconfig_client returns different objects each time out of pure convenience of the implementers and that the object is so cheap and there's no need to cache it. Just keep thread/process-safety in mind when refactoring. :) [SEP] [COMMENTER] The bug comes from a system limit of how many file streams can be opened. I tried to dig into the snakebite code to figure out exactly where it happens. I think it's in the thrift communication. The problem is that each client opens a new communication pipe, so if too many are used at the same time you'll hit the limit. If every HdfsTarget has the same client, they don't open multiple pipes. The following code does not trigger the bug: [CODE] The bug also does not get triggered if there's a chance to garbage collect between exist calls, which usually happens during normal luigi operation. It's only when you hold more HdfsTargets than your system limit on open files in memory at the same time and operate on them all that there's a problem. [SEP] [COMMENTER] I see clearly now. Thanks for investigating this. Naturally, I would happily review and accept a PR fixing this :) [SEP] [COMMENTER] Just wanted to check if there was a good reason not to cache the autoconfig client, maybe threading/multiprocessing issues? [SEP] [COMMENTER] I see, I unfortunately don't know. [SEP] [COMMENTER] I just ran some tests. It seems to be ok to persist the same client object across processes but not threads. Maybe we can make it safe by checking against threading.current_thread() to make sure we create a new one for each thread? [SEP] [COMMENTER] I suppose the reason it works across processes is because you don't share the variables or anything like that in Python, hence it actually creates multiple objects. I might be wrong though. ^^ Yea I think your solution sounds sane.

samples.title: Local scheduler does not prune automatically
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] Local scheduler does not prune automatically Take these script: a main task that depends on a task that only completes successfully after some time has passed since launch [CODE] run it with these configs to simplify testing/debugging: [CODE] If you run this with central scheduler, the dependency will fail initially, but after some it will be retried because the server calls scheduler.prune every minute: [CODE] and scheduler.prune causes rescheduling/retrying of the failed task (through triggering SimpleTaskState.prune) So eventually all work will be done successfully. However if you run this with the local scheduler (additional --local-scheduler), you end up in an endless loop because scheduler.prune is never called and the failed task never gets a chance on a retry. (FYI: I lost quite some time figuring all this out when I was examining the retry behaviour of Luigi with the local scheduler, assuming it would work the same as the central scheduler)

samples.title: hive table_exists should be case insensitive?
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] hive table_exists should be case insensitive? Any thoughts on this one? In [URL] (possibly here too, but we're not on CDH: [URL] Sometimes we have tables that are defined as capitalized, rather than lower case underscored names. These are easier to read in code if left capitalized, though hive is case insensitive, and will return them as lower case. E.g. when checking for an existing table table = 'FooBar', stdout will return with foobar and the test will fail This wasn't an issue in older versions, which just checked for string "does not exist" or "Table not found" in stdout. Would be easy to fix using return stdout and table.lower() in stdout or return stdout and table.lower() in stdout.lower() Let me know your thoughts on this. I can supply a pull request if necessary. Thanks, Lin. [SEP] [COMMENTER] Please do give a pull request, with an accompanying test if possible :)

samples.title: Matplotlib 3.2.1 savefig empty image when fig size matches data size exactly
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] Matplotlib 3.2.1 savefig empty image when fig size matches data size exactly ### Bug report **Bug summary** Saving a figure to an image file with the same pixel dimensions as data loaded with imshow does not work in 3.2.1, producing an empty image. If the dimensions are changed by even one pixel, normal behavior returns. This bug appeared in 3.2.1 and I have confirmed that it does not occur with 3.1.1. I noticed in the release notes for 3.2.0 that some changes were made to the default image interpolation; perhaps an unhandled condition was introduced here when no interpolation is required. However, I note that changing the interpolation mode had no effect on this bug. **Code for reproduction** [CODE] **Actual outcome** ![bug_image]( [URL] **Expected outcome** ![working_image]( [URL] Produced with matplotlib 3.1.1 **Matplotlib version** * Operating system: Linux version 5.6.13-arch1-1 * Matplotlib version: 3.2.1 * Matplotlib backend (print(matplotlib.get_backend())): TkAgg * Python version: 3.8.3 * Other libraries: numpy 1.18.4 Default system install on Arch linux package python-matplotlib-3.2.1-1-x86_64 [SEP] [COMMENTER] If you remove pad_inches=0 this works fine. This stopping working seems to bisect to a9b8b6e21d6ef1 which I think has more to do with extents than the image work. [URL] [SEP] [COMMENTER] Furthermore, if you do pad_inches=0.0000000001 it works fine, or the axes size to 0.999999 etc it works fine. So its definitely that you are hitting a precise integer somehow. But if you don't do the tight_bbox, or pad_inches, you get exactly what you want. [SEP] [COMMENTER] Thanks for the quick response and work-around. Hopefully it will be possible to get to the underlying issue. I don't recall the origin of the pad and bbox options in this code, though I would note that it is surprising that not specifying the pad_inches and bbox_inches options results in no padding in this case. From the documentation, the default value for pad_inches is 0.1 inch. [SEP] [COMMENTER] pad_inches only matters if you use bbox_inches='tight' [SEP] [COMMENTER] This was broken by [URL] which disables the draw methods on the renderer (because to compute the bounding boxes we don't need to actually draw turn the crank on the renderer methods, only the artist methods) but we never re-set the methods to their original values. This is not normally a problem as the tight bounding box is a different size that original size so the caching in [URL] misses and we get a new, un-patched renderer. However, in this case the figure is already the correct size so we hit the cache and get back the renderer with the draw methods stripped off which then results in an empty output. I can see a bunch of hacky ways to fix this, but not sure which one is the best. [SEP] [COMMENTER] It seems to me that draw_disabled=True should just make a new un-cached renderer? [SEP] [COMMENTER] The problem is that where we know if we have flagged it as draw_disabled (in backend bases) is not where we know if there is a cache or not (deep in the print_XYZ functions in the backends).

samples.title: Scatter produce multiple colors for a single RGB/RGBA input
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] Scatter produce multiple colors for a single RGB/RGBA input ### Bug report **Bug summary** Scatter [docs]( [URL] say that in order to provide a single RGB/RGBA color for all scatter elements, it is required to pass a 2D array with a single row for c argument input: When x/y shape is 3 and RGB is passed as a 2D array with a single row (e.g. c=[[1, 0.5, 0.05]]), or when x/y shape is 4 and RGBA is passed as a 2D array with a single row, the plot elements have several different colors (taken from the default colormap?) while all should have the same color. The problem occurs for both plt.scatter and ax.scatter calls, and also if c input is provided as a numpy array of shape (1,3) for RGB or (1,4) for RGBA instead of a nested list. **Code for reproduction** [CODE] **Actual outcome** ![840-mpl]( [URL] **Expected outcome** All points have the same color: RGB (1, 0.5, 0.05). **Matplotlib version** * Operating system: Linux * Matplotlib version: 3.2.1 * Matplotlib backend (print(matplotlib.get_backend())): agg * Python version: 3.7.3 Matplotlib installed through pip [SEP] [COMMENTER] This is a regression bug because it works as expected on 3.1.2 ![image]( [URL] [SEP] [COMMENTER] Bisects to 5a5c2f6ade2bb5cf92c9fde50ed07872e6b559f2, or #13959 by @efiring. I'm not actually sure why we want to flatten c since the docs say "If you want to specify the same RGB or RGBA value for all points, use a 2-D array with a single row.", but it should work if c is the same length as x/y. [SEP] [COMMENTER] IMO this is a pretty serious regression; it makes seaborn plots wrong with no straightforward way for the user to work around it. Could it please get a release critical tag? [SEP] [COMMENTER] If you know ahead of time that you want to specify an RGG(A) color not values to be colormapped you can use the color kwarg which has none of the ambiguity. See #17499 for fix. [SEP] [COMMENTER] seaborn.scatterplot can produce varying-color scatterplots though. It would be possible to check the shape of the color array after semantic mapping and conditionally dispatch to different matplotlib kwargs, but boy would it be messy... [SEP] [COMMENTER] We currently have that code in mpl and it _is_ rather messy (which is why it keeps breaking :/ )

samples.title: Surprising/changed axis limit (autoscale) behavior
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] Surprising/changed axis limit (autoscale) behavior ### Bug report **Bug summary** The interaction of setting axis limits and autoscaling changed in 3.2 with little guidance, introducing unexpected behavior that doesn't always make sense. **Code for reproduction** Here is a plot that looks very different on 3.1.2 and 3.2.1 [CODE] **Actual outcome** On 3.2.1 ![image]( [URL] **Expected outcome** On 3.1.2 ![image]( [URL] **Matplotlib version** * Operating system: macOS * Matplotlib version: 3.2.1 * Matplotlib backend (print(matplotlib.get_backend())): pylab inline * Python version: 3.7 [SEP] [COMMENTER] Is "autoscaling" actually documented anywhere? [URL] just says "turn autoscaling on or off" but that's a bit tautological: it's saying "function f does f". What effect does f have? [SEP] [COMMENTER] Bisects to 160de568e1 [URL] @anntzer any quick idea what's going on here? I didn't even know about the auto=None option. [SEP] [COMMENTER] [URL] is the method that actually does the rescaling and has a slightly better explaination. This also gets coupled with "margins" and "sticky edges" [URL] I suspect that this related to making the auto-scaling lazier. On master I get a flash of the wrong scaling before it goes to the correct limits but it is still broken an v3.2.x. I suspect the problem is that adding the scatter "arms" the rescale, but setting the ylim does not "disarm" it. If you force a f.canvas.draw() just before setting the ylim it works correctly. [SEP] [COMMENTER] This only happens w/ auto=None, which says to keep the autoscaling as it was before the set_ylim. So I'd argue the plot is correct since you are asking for the y-axis to be autoscaled, at least according to the docs. What did auto=None do before? Only autoscale only if more data is added? Thats super confusing in my opinion, but maybe seaborn has a use case? [SEP] [COMMENTER] No, I'm asking for the y axis limits to be (-.5, +.5) at this moment in time. I am also asking for this action not to change whether or not the plot will continue to autoscale in the future. Yes. My understanding, and the previous behavior, is that the auto parameter of set_{x,y}lim influences what happens in the future, not in response to that call. Why is that super confusing? If I am calling a method to set explicit limits, I don't think matplotlib should ignore my limits and autoscale to narrower values. [SEP] [COMMENTER] To be clear, there has been a change when auto=True as well. [CODE] 3.1.2: ![image]( [URL] 3.2.1: ![image]( [URL] [SEP] [COMMENTER] I guess [CODE] is what we need, can you confirm that works for you? [SEP] [COMMENTER] I agree, but then to autoscale if I add something new is surprising. I can see that its a useful feature, but it adds some complexity to the state of the plot that we have to keep track of, and obviously got dropped here. @anntzer, your patch appears to work for me. But I guess we need to test this flag better. [SEP] [COMMENTER] Well the reason seaborn uses auto=None is mostly for backwards compatibility, as it matches how ax.set{x,y}lim had previously worked. I recall the original change causing some pain that seemed best solved at the time by just preserving the old behavior, but I don't remember the details of the issues. [SEP] [COMMENTER] What I really want from explicit limits / autoscaling is something closer to a ratchet: I want to set limits that won't hide to-be-added data that lie outside of the limits but will also not contract when to-be-added data lie inside the limits (minus the margin). But I'd settle for some better/less tautological documentation for how autoscale is supposed to work :) [SEP] [COMMENTER] @mwaskom I agree, that would be great. Until such a dream is realized I found that I could get satisfactory results like so: [CODE] Full code + associated x_lim version lives here: [URL] [SEP] [COMMENTER] @ianhi cleaner to write that code as [CODE]

samples.title: “Size” ignored if placed before fontproperties
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] “Size” ignored if placed before fontproperties ### Bug report **Bug summary** In as.text, ax.set_title and all the functions realated to displaying characters on the figure, the arguement size is ignored if it is placed before fontproperties. **Code for reproduction** [CODE] This issue is replicated with ipython (%pylab) and jupyter-notebook #### **Actual outcome** [CODE] **Expected outcome** **Matplotlib version** * Operating system: win10 1909 * Matplotlib version: Version :3.1.2 , Build : py36_1 , conda-forge * Matplotlib backend (print(matplotlib.get_backend())): module://ipykernel.pylab.backend_inline * Python version: 3.6.10 * Jupyter version (if applicable): version 1.0.0 * Other libraries: conda install -c conda-forge matplotlib [SEP] [COMMENTER] Somehow update() gets called with both values even though Text() holds fontproperties as explicit parameter but size is handled via **kwargs. Since the passed arguments of update() are processed sequentially, order matters here. If fontproperties is used with a string, a FontProperties instance with non-given defaults (including size) is created. That will overwrite the explicit size in the second example. IMHO expicit font-related keywords like size should have precedence over fontproperties. This is a good first issue to dig into. Please include a test based on the above example to validate the fix. [SEP] [COMMENTER] However, if we get passed an actual FontProperties objects (not a string we implicitly up-convert) the precedence is not completely clear. I can see an argument for in that case the order of the kwargs mattering and the last one winning, however in the case of a string input (like this example) it is pretty clear that the size kwarg should have higher priority. Given that is is better to not have behavior like that depend on the exact types, size should always win. Which is a very long way of saying "I agree with @timhoffm". [SEP] [COMMENTER] I'd be happy to take a look at this if you wouldn't mind? From a quick dig around (and @timhoffm's comment) I think it's coming from Artist.update() [URL] The simplest solution I can think of would be to pop fontproperties from the props dict to ensure set_fontproperties is run before any of the other functions. It's not particularly elegant, but it would ensure that values given by the other kwargs would always take precedence. [SEP] [COMMENTER] See [URL] where I'm basically trying to get rid of this kind of kwargs reordering... I think the proper solution would be for FontProperties to properly keep unset properties as being, well, unset, and not overwriting already existing properties on the text object. Also, I guess this raises some other questions, e.g. in [CODE] should the second call reset the size to the rc default? (Note that in the base case here, passing family="SimHei" should work just fine, and will in fact work better if the font family contains a dash...) [SEP] [COMMENTER] Is anyone else currently working on this? If not I'll give it a try. [SEP] [COMMENTER] @RiazCharania go for it. The first step is to clarify the expected behavior. See [URL] and [URL] I that you provide a short summary defining the behavior for all cases before implementing. [SEP] [COMMENTER] We'll give it a try too

samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] Series groupby does not include zero or nan counts for all categorical labels, unlike DataFrame groupby #### Steps to reproduce [CODE] #### Problem description When performing a groupby on categorical columns, categories with empty groups should be present in output. That is, the multi-index of the object returned by count() should contain the Cartesian product of all the labels of the first categorical column ("treatment" in the example above) and the second categorical column ("type") by which the grouping was performed. The behavior in cell [3] above is correct. But in cell [4], after obtaining a pandas.core.groupby.SeriesGroupBy object, the series returned by the count() method does not have entries for all levels of the "type" categorical. #### Expected Output The output from cell [4] should be equivalent to this output, with length 6, and include values for the index values (C, B) and (T, C). [CODE] #### Workaround Perform column access after calling count(): [CODE] #### Output of `pd.show_versions()` [CODE] [SEP] [COMMENTER] Worth noting that .size() also drops the 0-counts for both Series and GroupBy. I know size and count are different but I would expect them to make the same indexing decision here. [SEP] [COMMENTER] The desired output is the Series one. You definitely don't want the cartesian product of your groupby cols. The dataframe one is wrong or at least I can't see why you would want that as the default behaviour. You will explode memory. [SEP] [COMMENTER] And to be more constructive, I would imagine in your use case you want some sort of reindex first using something like this stuff: [URL] [SEP] [COMMENTER] This issue was created before the introduction of the observed keyword in groupby(), but it seems to me that with the default of observed=False all categories should be present in the output as @nmusolino expects. On first sight this is true for most aggregations, but count() seems to be an exception: [CODE] So I do think this is a bug that's still present. [SEP] [COMMENTER] A severe warning should be issued when this gets pushed in the what's new as it will cause memory blow-ups if anyone is not using the observed=True non-default on sparsely observed high-arity categoricals. [SEP] [COMMENTER] @cottrell would welcome a memory benchmark in asv to see for sure [SEP] [COMMENTER] @jreback is there a way of doing that though without killing the test framework? I don't think it is a test-worthy case really ... I mean simply that if you have 20k rows indexed by three cols with arity 10k x 10k x 10k, you will get a cube ravelled to 1e12 rows with the default settings. Setting observed=True gives < 20k rows. The new default is fine, probably best folks learn to turn off the cartesian expansion. But could hit people if they upgrade old code. [SEP] [COMMENTER] @cottrell you can show a simliar effect in a *much* smaller df, e.g i don't see why 10 x 10 x 10 wouldn't work [SEP] [COMMENTER] I don't think we are talking about the same thing. A reasonable test to block this default change would have been any test that fails due to explosion of dimensions when observed=False. The test would need to run and try to produce an array too large to compute. If the test was runnable with observed=False, then it would have been an invalid test. As the new default is in, there is nothing to block anymore and this kind of test has no value in the current state. It is probably the good state since now anyway since everything must be explicit in high arity cases. Below is the example above in the two cases for ref. ![image]( [URL]

samples.title: Problem with passing in a single tuple in custom_repr= ()
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] Problem with passing in a single tuple in custom_repr= () I was trying it on a toy example and just wanted to pass in one tuple like (list, print_list_size) to custom_repr= () with print_list_size being the example used in the readme file: def print_list_size(l): return 'list(size={})'.format(len(l)) There was an exception:" TypeError: cannot unpack non-iterable function object." I think the problem lies in that custom_repr is a tuple of tuples thus when it has only one element it won't be unpacked successfully. I changed custom_repr = [ ] and then put in the tuple (list, print_list_size) and it worked. Or, put an extra , in the tuple such like this: @pysnooper.snoop(custom_repr = ( (list, print_list_size), )) Just a small issue but hopefully this would be beneficial for other people. [SEP] [COMMENTER] @cool-RR I can't be bothered with an actual PR, but something like this in the Tracer class should do: if custom_repr and callable(custom_repr[0]): custom_repr = [custom_repr] FYI @DavidykZhao [snoop]( [URL] will show you the len() of most things out of the box in addition to the usual repr. [SEP] [COMMENTER] @alexmojaki That's something I am thinking about as well. Thanks for the suggestion. Looking forward to new features.

samples.title: "Named Response Middleware" executed in wrong order
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] "Named Response Middleware" executed in wrong order **Describe the bug** PR [URL] Introduced "named response middlware" that is, middleware which is only executed in a given request context. For example a blueprint middleware is only executed on a route which is defined in _that_ blueprint. There was a copy+paste error in the register_named_middleware function, here: [URL] When registering a "response" middleware, they are supposed to be added to the left in reverse. So appendleft() should be used instead of append(). The correct behavior is seen in the normal register_middleware function. **Code snippet** See these two examples, the first using normal middleware, and the second using named middleware: [CODE] vs: [CODE] **Expected behavior** See the first snippet prints "1 2 3 6 5 4" (correct) but the second snippet prints "1 2 3 4 5 6". samples.title: [suggest ] `response.follow` should raise a exception when called on None or an empty string, instead of crawling the current page again
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] [suggest ] response.follow should raise a exception when called on None or an empty string, instead of crawling the current page again response.follow will raise a exception when url='' or none in stead of crawl the (base) page itself again. none will use follow to crawl the source(base) page again right? all parsers will be passed without warning if that way. thanks [SEP] [COMMENTER] Hi @NewUserHa - I re-worded the title, to more clearly match what I _think_ you're suggesting. Can you confirm that I understood you correctly? This does look like an odd behaviour, and I can think of plenty of ways that a None could creep in there in normal circumstances, sadly. I don't think this should be the expected behaviour; maybe for special pseudo-URLs like "#" or ".", but not IMO for the empty string or None? [SEP] [COMMENTER] If url is '', Scrapy should follow the same page, this is an intended behavior. Doing otherwise would be non-standard, and can break a lot of things. When is url None? Could you please give an example? [SEP] [COMMENTER] Hi @kmike, take this contrived example: [CODE] Dupefilter should catch these cases, probably. But if Dupefilter is disabled for some reason and custom logic enabled, then it's possible for this to cause unexpected behaviour. Good to know about empty-string being expected behaviour. [SEP] [COMMENTER] Right, I would expect response.follow to raise an exception if None is passed, +1 to fix that. From docs: [CODE] ^^ None is not one of accepted values. [SEP] [COMMENTER] @cathalgarvey yeah thanks. @kmike it may be intended. but in the most situation, I suppose none intense to crawl the current page again just because of extract_first() return an empty string/link. and it's redundancy if check if it's empty every time I believe. ( even if the dupefilter works well, but it will hide the un-except result since few ppl will only check error message beside dupe message. PLUS the doc/tutorial never mention this problem) thanks if you also fix empty string or note in doc [SEP] [COMMENTER] Hey @NewUserHa - are you interested in contributing a fix for the issue? I might also mark it as "Good First Issue" otherwise, because I think this should be an easy enough fix: 1. Add a test case for passing None as an argument to Response.follow, expect it to throw a TypeError 1. Write code to check for None and throw that error when it happens in Response.follow. Or, better still, write a check for all expected types, and throw TypeError on any other type. e.g. isinstance(url, (str, Selector, Link)). [SEP] [COMMENTER] @cathalgarvey currently response.follow throws ValueErrors for bad url argument; it makes sense for unsupported Selectors, though I'm not sure throwing it for SelectorList is the way to go. [SEP] [COMMENTER] I assumed from the above paste, that SelectorList is an unsupported Type, so I reckon that's a TypeError (even though I see how that might confuse some users because its API is so similar to Selector). But, throwing a ValueError for Selectors that can't reasonably be "followed" makes sense. Though, that raises questions like "Should we allow things with src attributes to be followed, not just a[href] elements?" [SEP] [COMMENTER] Right - we started conservative, only with elements at first, then added elements in next Scrapy version. Following anything with src requires more discussion, and this discussion haven't started. I can see some disadvantages, e.g. a selector doesn't select explicitly, a website uses an image for missing links, and selector selects this image, image URL would be followed instead of a link. It can be also more common to use file or media pipeline to download content linked via src attribute. So ideally, it should start with a use case from a real project, where code can be simplified if response.follow supports selectors with src. [SEP] [COMMENTER] @cathalgarvey thanks, and I created two pull requests. #3131 #3132 [SEP] [COMMENTER] Hi @NewUserHa - We might as well keep the implementation and the tests together, could you merge one patch into the other patch? Thanks, will review these shortly. :) [SEP] [COMMENTER] @cathalgarvey done. #3131 [SEP] [COMMENTER] Fixed by [URL] [SEP] [COMMENTER] hmm, can the empty string url be discussed again? [SEP] [COMMENTER] @NewUserHa from my point of view, it is clear that we shouldn't raise an exception for empty URLs in response.follow or response.urljoin. Clicking on a link with empty URL redirects you to the same page in browsers, and joining with an empty URL is a perfectly standard operation. Doing otherwise would be non-standard. The only argument so far against current way of handling "" is that response.follow will return a request to the same page. But I see it as an expected behavior, and something which can be desired. For example, you may do a POST request to submit a form to the same page. Or a website may require to re-send a request with different cookies. Or you may be using scrapy-splash, where URL fragment matters, and "" is not really the same page as a current page. Or you really want to send the request twice, e.g. to check if page changes. If we start raising an error for an empty string, it'd break all these use cases. [SEP] [COMMENTER] well, the latter examples do make sense. also, the None is the unexcepted behavior reason of what I wanted to say. thanks [SEP] [COMMENTER] Thanks for the report @NewUserHa and for the PR fixing the bug! Sorry that the empty-string behaviour is a problem for you. Scrapy often has to match Browser behaviour, even if that behaviour is a bit odd. I hope you find a clean way to work around the issue in your project. [SEP] [COMMENTER] oh, I meant "It's None that the bad guy caused a situation what I wanted to say" and thanks for the explanation from the rest part of your reply

samples.title: RuntimeError: inconsistent AsyncHTTPClient cache
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] RuntimeError: inconsistent AsyncHTTPClient cache Tornado version: 5.1.1 Running the following code causes RuntimeError: inconsistent AsyncHTTPClient cache [CODE] Error log: [CODE] Since the error is ignored, it may be harmless to the program. But the error message may confuse some new users, so I think it should be fixed. [SEP] [COMMENTER] This looks like a subtle consequence of the interpreter shutdown sequence that I don't fully understand. The (synchronous) HTTPClient has references to an IOLoop and an AsyncHTTPClient, and there is a global WeakKeyDictionary that maps from the IOLoop to the AsyncHTTPClient. It looks like the GC is deciding that all three objects are garbage at the same time, and it clears the weakref to the IOLoop before calling the HTTPClient's destructor. We could relax this assertion so it doesn't fire here (it's not strictly necessary, it's just there to help catch certain hypothetical bugs), but this highlights the weird environment that __del__ methods run in. The synchronous HTTPClient is the only class in Tornado with a __del__ method; everything else requires an explicit close() if there is any cleanup to be done. Maybe it should be changed for consistency?

samples.title: ioloop: PeriodicCallback executes too often on windows
samples.dialogue_text:
[REPORTER] ioloop: PeriodicCallback executes too often on windows ## Here is the code: import math import logging from crontab import CronTab from tornado.ioloop import PeriodicCallback, IOLoop logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG, format='%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s') class CronTabCallback(PeriodicCallback): def __init__(self, callback, schedule): self._callback = callback self._crontab = CronTab(schedule) super(CronTabCallback, self).__init__(self.run, self._calc_callbacktime()) def _calc_callbacktime(self, now=None): return math.ceil(self._crontab.next(now)) * 1000.0 def run(self): return self._callback() def _schedule_next(self): self.callback_time = self._calc_callbacktime() logging.info('calc ---------------------') logging.info('delay %s' % self.callback_time) logging.info('last execute %s' % self._next_timeout) last = self._next_timeout super(CronTabCallback, self)._schedule_next() if last == self._next_timeout: logging.error('error !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!') logging.info('current: %s' % self.io_loop.time()) logging.info('calc next: %s' % self._next_timeout) logging.info('--------------------------
') def crontab(schedule): def decorator(func): CronTabCallback(func, schedule).start() return func return decorator @crontab('*/1 * * * *') def run(): logging.info('execute ... 
') if __name__ == '__main__': IOLoop.current().start() ## Here is the console log 2018-03-30 11:33:00,311 - asyncio - DEBUG - Using selector: SelectSelector 2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - calc --------------------- 2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - delay 60000.0 2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - last execute 1522380780.3169544 2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - current: 1522380780.3169544 2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - **calc next: 1522380840.3169544** 2018-03-30 11:33:00,316 - root - INFO - -------------------------- **2018-03-30 11:34:00,313** - root - INFO - execute ... 2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - calc --------------------- 2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - delay 60000.0 2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - last execute 1522380840.3169544 2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - ERROR - error !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! 2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - current: 1522380840.3139544 2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - calc next: 1522380840.3169544 2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - -------------------------- 2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - execute ... 2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - calc --------------------- 2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - delay 60000.0 2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - last execute 1522380840.3169544 2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - current: 1522380840.3189545 2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - calc next: 1522380900.3169544 2018-03-30 11:34:00,318 - root - INFO - -------------------------- ## Environment: OS: windows 7 Python: python 3.6 Dependent library: crontab 0.22.0 Tornado 4.5.1 python2 (ok) python3(ok) Tornado 5.0.1 python2 (ok) python3 (linux ok, window has issue) [SEP] [COMMENTER] ## The following code will be executed twice every minute import logging from tornado.ioloop import PeriodicCallback, IOLoop logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG, format='%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s') def run(): logging.info('execute ... 
') if __name__ == '__main__': PeriodicCallback(run, 60000).start() IOLoop.current().start() [SEP] [COMMENTER] Standard disclaimer: Windows is not a fully-supported platform for Tornado. I think this is because time.time() on windows is very low resolution, and there is a bug in the logic in PeriodicCallback._schedule_next if we can complete our callback faster than the clock changes. I'm not sure why it doesn't happen on python 2, though. [SEP] [COMMENTER] The same python3 environment 4.5 why there is no problem, there is a problem with 5.0.1, is this the problem of the operating system? [SEP] [COMMENTER] I'm not sure. The big change in 5.0 is that on python 3 we always use the asyncio event loop. So the asyncio loop is acting differently from the old tornado loop somehow. Maybe it's because asyncio uses a different (monotonic) clock internally but tornado is using time.time() which is subject to ntp adjustments. Those shouldn't be that frequent, though. In any case, it definitely looks like PeriodicCallback has a bug when the callback completes faster than the resolution of the clock, so it should be fixed there. It's not windows' fault, but the fact that it's only a problem on windows is why it hasn't been noticed before. [SEP] [COMMENTER] I think the solution to this problem is to find the root of the problem first, and then solve it from the root, rather than avoid it by modifying the logic of PeriodicCallback._schedule_next. I don't know why it's specific, so I can't solve it, but I will continue to pay attention to it. Because my English is not good, these are translated by means of translation tools. I hope it doesn't bring trouble to you. Thank you for reading. [SEP] [COMMENTER] OK, so looking at these timestamps, the first time through the loop (For clarity, I will only use the three digits left and right of the decimal point): [CODE] It is now 780.316, so the next run should be 60s later, at 840.316. But the next run wakes up early, at 840.313: 2018-03-30 11:34:00,313 - root - INFO - current: 1522380840.3139544 Since this is less than the "next" time, we use the same "next" time for the following run. In Tornado's IOLoop, we perform an additional check of the current time before scheduling a callback: [URL] If your system clock moves backwards, this will effectively delay the callback, which hides the time adjustment from higher levels unless it occurs between the IOLoop scheduling the callback and it running. Asyncio does a [similar check of the current time]( [URL] but *it uses a different clock*. Specifically, Tornado uses time.time(), which is subject to NTP adjustments, while asyncio uses time.monotonic, which is not (although it may be slewed in an effort to minimize discrepancies between the two clocks; this happens on linux according to PEP 418). So I think I was wrong to blame the low precision of windows clocks. This can happen on any system where time.time() runs slower than time.monotonic(). Linux can avoid minor differences here by slewing the monotonic clock, but when larger clock adjustments are required it can happen there too. We need to either remove this if or give it an else. We always want to move the time forward. I think just removing the if will do the right thing. [URL] [SEP] [COMMENTER] I think I have a fix for this in #2338. Can you try it out? [SEP] [COMMENTER] I tested it and it will not be executed twice now. Because each time will be a little bit earlier, after a longer period, the number of executions will be less than expected. Self._next_timeout = current_time + callback_time_sec Should this code be written like this? Self._next_timeout += callback_time_sec [SEP] [COMMENTER] The problem is that there are two different clocks (time.time() and time.monotonic()) running at two different speeds. Which one should we use? Tornado used to use time.time() for everything; asyncio has always used time.monotonic(). What do these do on your machine? [CODE] [CODE] I think that you'll see these are also running slightly faster than once per minute according to time.time(). (but they're running on schedule according to time.monotonic()) If the clock is moving backwards just a little bit because of the difference between time.time() and time.monotonic(), then self._next_timeout += callback_time_sec. But if the clock moves backwards for any other reason, or if it moves backwards by a lot, it's better to reset to current_time + callback_time_sec. I think since it's impossible to tell why the clock is moving backwards (or which clock is more accurate if they're different), it's better to use current_time + callback_time_sec. [SEP] [COMMENTER] I've changed my mind about this. When there's a difference between the two, it's generally going to be because time.time() is getting synchronized via NTP with an external source, so it will be more accurate. I'll change this to your suggestion of self._next_timeout += callback_time_sec. If there's a large jump backwards, then we may have to wait a while before the next run of the callback. But A) large backwards jumps shouldn't really happen and B) depending on when the jump occurs, there's not much we can do about it anyway (we can only reliably detect backwards jumps that occur during the execution of the callback. In between callbacks we have to rely on what the event loop itself does)

samples.title: Synchronous HTTPClient leaks memory in 5.0.1
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] Synchronous HTTPClient leaks memory in 5.0.1 The synchronous HTTPClient leaks memory for each request made since upgrading to Tornado 5.0.1. [CODE] Running this script for 3 minutes in docker (python:3.6-stretch): - tornado==4.5.3: 18.52MiB / 1.952GiB - tornado==5.0.1: 350.28MiB / 1.952GiB Note, I cannot seem to reproduce the issue on macOS. [SEP] [COMMENTER] about httpclient.HTTPClient() I can't execute **http_client = httpclient.HTTPClient()** in tornado==5.0 running this will throw Exception this： Traceback (most recent call last): File "/Users/zhonghao/anaconda3/envs/tornado_test/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tornado/httpclient.py", line 85, in __del__ self.close() File "/Users/zhonghao/anaconda3/envs/tornado_test/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tornado/httpclient.py", line 89, in close if not self._closed: AttributeError: 'HTTPClient' object has no attribute '_closed' but in tornado==4.5.2 it works fine, this issue on macOS，can you tell me why？ [SEP] [COMMENTER] Thanks for the report! I can easily reproduce this on macOS (with python 3.6. the issue doesn't occur with python 2. Which version were you using in your macos test?). The leak occurs when an IOLoop is created and destroyed, so if you create a single HTTPClient and reuse it, the leak goes away. The cause of the leak is [IOLoop._ioloop_for_asyncio]( [URL] which is a WeakKeyDictionary. However, there is a strong reference from the IOLoop to the asyncio loop, which prevents the asyncio loop from ever being garbage collected. A fix for this particular issue is easy: when the IOLoop is closed, we can break up the reference cycle. But this still effectively turns the WeakKeyDictionary into a strongly-referenced dict. Is there a way to rearrange the reference graph so that asyncio event loops remain garbage collectable in the normal way?

samples.title: ITV Hub .ttml subs fail to convert to .srt
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] ITV Hub .ttml subs fail to convert to .srt - [x] I've **verified** and **I assure** that I'm running youtube-dl **2017.09.11** - [x] At least skimmed through the [README]( [URL] **most notably** the [FAQ]( [URL] and [BUGS]( [URL] sections - [x] [Searched]( [URL] the bugtracker for similar issues including closed ones ### What is the purpose of your *issue*? - [x] Bug report (encountered problems with youtube-dl) Standalone youtube-dl.exe v2017.09.11 on Windows Vista SP2 32bit, fully patched. Command issued: [CODE] Verbose log is quite lengthy, instead of being inserted in the body of this issue, it's been attached: [ITVHub_VLog.txt]( [URL] FWIW, the failure part reads: [CODE] Hopefully this is something that can be fixed... Many thanks for your ongoing efforts :+1: [SEP] [COMMENTER] #12909 should fix this problem. [SEP] [COMMENTER] Thanks to @remitamine, this will be fixed in the next version. [SEP] [COMMENTER] @yan12125 & @remitamine Unfortunately, latest release **2017.09.15** **has not fixed this original issue** , though now a different error is printed; youtube-dl command used: [CODE] Command prompt window excerpt: [CODE] _Please re-open this_ Many thanks in anticipation... [SEP] [COMMENTER] Sorry I didn't check carefully. It should really be fixed in the next version: (3869028ffb6be6ab719e5cf1004276dfdfd1216d) [CODE] [SEP] [COMMENTER] @yan12125 wrote: Hi I hope you're well ! No need to apologise; as ever, your constant efforts and user support is greatly appreciated (this goes to the whole team BTW :+1: ) So, after a wait of several days, next (latest) version **2017.09.24** was released and I was eager to try it out: [CODE] As expected, now the subs conversion from .ttml to .srt _completes successfully_; for the record, out of the major software players on the Windows platform, only VLC 3.0.0-git appears to have native support for the .ttml (dfxp) subs format; but I usually use MPC-BE / MPC-HC / PotPlayer, which all require the conversion to .srt, hence the original issue that was filed... Trying the generated .srt file with MPC-BE, I almost immediately saw there must be a **flaw somewhere in the conversion from .ttml -> .srt**, which is demonstrated as the following issue: 1. When a sub contains two (or more) lines and the **font colour is white**, everything looks OK: ![white_2lines]( [URL] 2. However, in every **two-line subtitle** where **font colour !=white** (even with different colours between lines), **the font size in the second line is noticeably bigger that the first one**: ![green_2lines]( [URL] ![yellow_2lines]( [URL] ![blue_2lines]( [URL] ![2colours_2lines]( [URL] Unfortunately, I know nothing about Python, this is not something I can fix myself and submit a PR, so again it should be investigated and hopefully remedied by a dev... A personal comment/suggestion: I see that the .ttml subs is the first item to be downloaded by youtube-dl, folowed by the video itself, the subs conversion is the last step; maybe consider putting the subs conversion right after fetching the .ttml file (this would avoid waiting for the video download to complete before grabbing the .srt file)? In a related matter, ITV Hub usually first publish a fresh video without the accompanying subs, these may (or in rare cases may not) be added at a later stage; AFAICS, there's not a **--subs-only** switch in youtube-dl so as to fetch only the subs once they become available; reading the docs it would appear --skip-download --write-sub --convert-subs=srt -k should be able to achieve the same thing, but, sadly, I am left with only the .ttml subs (because subs conversion follows after video fetch... ). For your convenience and further testing, I am attaching here both .ttml+.srt subs produced by latest youtube-dl: [ITV_Subs_bug.zip]( [URL] And I'm not sure if it's at all helpful, but here's a [link]( [URL] how get-flash-videos handles the subs conversion in Perl... A million thanks for this excellent tool, along with your consideration for disabled people (my sister is hard of hearing, so relies on .srt subs...). [SEP] [COMMENTER] Yep that's another bug (caused by me!). Please follow #9073 for updates. For the issue of larger texts, could you try the SRT on more players? At the first glance, the transformed srt for this video seems correct. [SEP] [COMMENTER] Neither VLC nor mpv have such problem. Most likely MPC does not support nested tags properly. ![image]( [URL] ![image]( [URL] [SEP] [COMMENTER] ... Thank you both for your input/suggestions. yan12125 wrote: Well, being on a Sunday I had some more free time to check, and: **MPC-HC** (stable release and latest beta) exhibits the same behaviour as described in [URL] in default video dimensions/maximised window/full screen, second line of coloured subtitle has larger fonts. **MPC-BE** (my preferred video player), being a fork of MPC-HC, as expected has the same "issue"; let me just say for the record that I've been using MPC-HC for almost a decade (WinXP/Vista/7), for the last year or so switched to MPC-BE (on Vista/7), and it's the first time a flavour of .srt subtitles isn't rendered properly... 😕 **PotPlayer** is a mixed bag case; the .srt file produced by yt-dl has the "issue" I reported when viewing the video in default dimensions, but the "issue" goes away in maximised window/full screen modes; even more bizarrely, this "issue" goes completely away, in all possible window modes, if I make the subtitle fonts **bold** (???). So, using PotPlayer with bold fonts is one solution I could change to... dstftw wrote: I do have a portable installation of VLC 2.2.6 on this Vista laptop and can confirm; however, I mostly use VLC as an audio stream recorder rather than a video viewer; be that as it may, **when I full-screen VLC with this certain type of .srt file produced by youtube-dl, the subtitles remain extremely small**: ![fs]( [URL] Again, to overcome this flaw in VLC and read subs in full-screen I have to go into its advanced options and unselect subtitle formatting (resulting in monochrome subs 👎) ... To conclude, _I do insist the ITV .srt subs produced by yt-dl have a (small) flaw_; you, OTOH, state: and (blaming in essence the player...). I'm not a coder so can't challenge your claims, in fact I'm overly grateful for this app; your coding time is probably better spent fixing other crucial yt-dl issues than this small subtitles handicap... If others are also OK with current status, I personally will further process those .srt subs with SubtitleEdit (or similar) to strip formatting and create a look of them I'm more comfortable with... Again, many thanks!

samples.title: --match-filter parsing with spaces or dashes
samples.dialogue_text: [REPORTER] --match-filter parsing with spaces or dashes How do I use --match-filter to match this youtube channel? Does it support spaces or hyphens in the string? This is the uploader and uploader_ID from the json dump for one of the videos. [CODE] For uploader, it will either not match spaces properly (breaking them into separate tokens) or just not match. [CODE] For uploader_id, I get this error: [CODE] I know I can specify the channel in the video URL, but I want to use my subscription list and pick the video format depending on the channel. I don't want to unsubscribe from these channels. I need to exclude those channels with the last catch-all download line in the script using match-format "uploader != xxxx & uploader != xxxx & uploader != xxxx " Here is that part of my script: [CODE] [SEP] [COMMENTER] It looks like a simple change to the regex will add this functionality. I don't know what the impact is to the rest of the project, though. Change line 1930 in utils.py from [CODE] to [CODE] This will allow whitespaces and dashes to be considered part of a strval. Since the input is already broken by & before it gets here, there shouldn't be any problem with the function knowing what part is what. [SEP] [COMMENTER] I've had a similar problem, but instead of '-', '_' was causing an exception. So the regex should be: (?P (?![0-9.])[a-z0-9A-Z/s/-]*) EDIT: Sometimes ids start with a number. Currently, when excluding such ids with id != , **NOTHING** will pass the filter. So to decide whether a value should be parsed as integer or string, strings should always start and end with '. So maybe something like: (?P \'[a-z0-9A-Z/s/-]*\')
